coredata.dc:title: Structure of deuteride of a null matrix Ti–Mo alloy studied by complementation of neutron and X-ray powder diffraction
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2006 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, E.
Yuan, X.Z.
Liu, S.
Guo, L.P.
Sun, K.
coredata.dc:description: The detailed structural information of the deuteride of a null matrix Ti–Mo alloy containing 33at.% of Mo is studied by complementation of the neutron and X-ray powder diffraction techniques. The deuteride containing ∼1.8 deuterium atoms per alloy atom forms a titanium δ-hydride type of structure with deuterium atoms in the tetrahedral interstitial sites. The line broadening analysis of the deuteride phase suggests that the deuterization of the alloy causes strong lattice deformation effects, and the micro-strain is likely to be dislocation induced. In comparison with the deformation of the host Ti–Mo alloy matrix lattice, a relaxation effect is observed for the deformation of the interstitial deuterium sublattice.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal hydrides
Crystal structure
Neutron diffraction
X-ray diffraction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Basic crystal structure and deuterium occupancy of the deuteride
Deformation in interstitial deuterium sublattice and host alloy lattice

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Rietveld fits to the X-ray and neutron diffraction patterns of the deuteride of 33at.% Mo Ti–Mo alloy collected at room temperature. Data points are shown as ‘×’ and the calculation as a solid line. The difference profiles for the refinements along with reflection markers for the indexed peaks are shown below each pattern.

The structural refinement results for (Ti0.67Mo0.33)D1.80 based on space group
F m
3 ¯
m

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The lattice parameter a =4.407(3)Å. The line broadening was corrected using a dislocation induced micro-strain model for cubic symmetry [10]. The numbers in parentheses are estimated standard deviations in the last significant figure of refined parameters.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The site occupancy of D is constrained to be a concentration of 1.8.

article.ce:title: Structure of deuteride of a null matrix Ti–Mo alloy studied by complementation of neutron and X-ray powder diffraction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: SYNL, Institute of Metal Research, Chinese Academy of Science, 72 WenHua Road, Shenhe District, Shenyang 110016, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: China Institute of Atomic Energy, P.O. Box 275(30), Beijing 102413, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of High Energy Physics, Chinese Academy of Science, P.O. Box 918, Beijing 100049, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The detailed structural information of the deuteride of a null matrix Ti–Mo alloy containing 33at.% of Mo is studied by complementation of the neutron and X-ray powder diffraction techniques. The deuteride containing ∼1.8 deuterium atoms per alloy atom forms a titanium δ-hydride type of structure with deuterium atoms in the tetrahedral interstitial sites. The line broadening analysis of the deuteride phase suggests that the deuterization of the alloy causes strong lattice deformation effects, and the micro-strain is likely to be dislocation induced. In comparison with the deformation of the host Ti–Mo alloy matrix lattice, a relaxation effect is observed for the deformation of the interstitial deuterium sublattice.

ce:section.ce:para: The hydrogenation of the α-Ti has been systematically studied for decades [1]. α-Ti can absorb significant amount of hydrogen to form a fluorite-type structure δ phase hydride of TiH1.5–2.0 [2]. The hydride precipitation causes strong embrittlement due to a large volume expansion of about 20% in the phase transitions. The high temperature β-Ti phase has a bcc structure. Mo is a common β-Ti stabilizing element. In a large section of the phase diagram, Ti–Mo alloy form bcc solid solutions [3]. Like α-Ti, which form stable hydrides with very low equilibrium pressures, the β phase Ti–Mo alloy can also absorb a large amount of hydrogen to form stable δ phase hydride at moderate pressures and temperatures, and the hydrides have relatively higher strength [4]. These properties and their potential applications have been noticed by several researchers, and some studies have been carried out for the hydrogenation and dehydrogenation of the Ti–Mo alloy [5–7].

However, in comparison with some other hydrides of Ti alloys, e.g. Ti–Fe–H and Ti–Mn–H systems, the studies on the hydrides of Ti–Mo alloy are still insufficient, especially on the detailed studies of the structures of the hydrides. We have noticed that Ti has a negative coherent neutron scattering length of −3.370fm, whereas Mo has a positive scattering length of 6.715fm. The Ti–Mo alloy containing 33at.% Mo is a bcc solid solution, which will have a zero resultant scattering amplitude and form a null matrix alloy for coherent elastic neutron scattering. For the neutron diffraction studies of the deuteride of this alloy, the coherent elastic neutron scattering of the matrix lattice of the alloying Ti–Mo atoms will be screened, whereas the scattering effect of the sublattice of the interstitial D atoms could be significantly amplified. Therefore the sole structural information of the deuterium sublattice in the deuteride could be revealed by the neutron diffraction technique. On the other hand, the X-ray diffraction could provide the structural information of the matrix lattice of the heavier Ti and Mo atoms in the alloy. Following this idea, we have studied the structure of the deuteride of the null matrix Ti–33at.% Mo alloy by exploiting complementation of the neutron and X-ray powder diffraction techniques. This paper reports our findings in the studies.

The 99.9% pure Ti and Mo metals were arc-melted, and then annealed in argon at 900°C for 72h to make Ti–33at.% Mo alloy sample. The loss during the sample preparation was less than 1wt.%. X-ray diffraction analysis suggests that the sample has a single phase of bcc structure with the lattice parameter of a =3.211(2)Å. No Bragg reflection peaks were observed in the neutron diffraction measurement of the alloy sample. The sample of ∼7g was deuterated using 99.9% pure deuterium gas in a Sieverts-type hydrogenator. The sample was activated by heating to ∼200°C at 150kPa until the observation of deuterium absorption. After activation and initial deuterization at room temperature, the sample was repeatedly heated to ∼200°C and cooled down to room temperature at atmospheric deuterium pressure until that the deuterium in the sample has saturated at 150kPa. A deuterium content of D/M =1.85±0.02 for the sample was estimated by measuring the pressure drop in the hydrogenator. The deuterated powder was air poisoned, and put in a 9mm diameter vanadium container for the neutron diffraction measurement. To examine the possible change of the sample due to the loss of hydrogen, the oxidation and/or nitridation after being exposed to the air, the deuterium, as well as oxygen and nitrogen contents of the sample are tested after the measurements by a LECO RH-404 hydrogen determinator and a LECO TC-436 oxygen and nitrogen determinator using the inert gas fusion principal. The hydrogen and nitrogen are measured by thermal conductivity, and oxygen is measured as carbon dioxide by an infrared detector. The accuracies of the measurements are 1% of the contents for oxygen and nitrogen and 3% for deuterium, respectively.

A neutron powder diffractometer at China Institute of Atomic Energy was used for neutron diffraction. The diffractometor has a Cu monochromator, take-off angle of 2θ=54° and fitted with 4 He3 detector spaced 3° apart. The measurement was performed with a neutron wavelength of 1.159Å. The scattering angle was 10–105° 2θ in 0.1° steps, with 11000 monitor counts per step. The deuteride sample was then taken for X-ray diffraction study. A Panalytical X′ Pert Pro MPD diffractometer with Kα Cu radiation was used for X-ray diffraction measurements. The scattering angle is 10–110° 2θ in 0.0167° steps of 120s per step. The sintered rutile (TiO2) cylinder (13mm diameter and 13mm height) samples were used as a standard for the calibration of instrumental line widths of both neutron and X-ray diffractometers.

Structural refinements were conducted by Rietveld analysis using a program modified from LHPM [8,9]. A dislocation-induced lattice deformation model based on the theories of Ryaboshapka and Tikhonov [10] has been implemented into the micro-strain line broadening correction of the program. In the refinement, the profiles are described by Voigt function. The refined parameters were: global parameters (diffractometer zero, the coefficients of a polynomial background), scale factors, lattice parameters, asymmetry parameters, preferred orientation parameters, line broadening parameters and isotropic thermal parameters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Basic crystal structure and deuterium occupancy of the deuteride
ce:section.ce:para: The X-ray and neutron diffraction patterns collected at room temperature for the deuteride sample of the 33at.% Mo Ti–Mo alloy fitted using Rietveld refinement are shown in Fig. 1 . Since the patterns were measured using different wavelengths in the two techniques, the scattering vector (Q =4π sin θ/λ) instead of the scattering angle has been used for comparison. The related structural analysis results are summarized in Table 1 .

As shown in Fig. 1, the peaks corresponding to a fcc unit cell can be identified and fitted by a structure similar to that of the δ-hydride of Ti metal in these patterns. In the δ-hydride type of structure under space group
F m
3 ¯
m
, the Ti and Mo atoms are randomly located in the 0,0,0 and other three equivalent positions, whereas the deuterium atoms can be situated in the tetragonal interstitial sites of 1/4,1/4,1/4 and other seven equivalent positions. The main influence of the Ti and Mo atoms on the neutron diffraction pattern has been the raise of the (111) peak at Q =24.7nm−1. Since there is no obvious peak at this position, it means that the scattering effects of the two metal elements have basically been counterbalanced. Consequently, the Ti and Mo atoms can be totally removed from the calculation with little influence on the quality of the refinement of the neutron pattern. This phenomenon thus confirms the null matrix effects of the host Ti–Mo alloy lattice. On the other hand, the exact total occupancies of the deuterium atoms at the tetragonal interstitial sites will not be derived from the refinement of the neutron diffraction pattern due to this effect. The deuterium content measured by the hydrogen determinator (calibrated by Zr deuterides) is D/M =1.80±0.05. Therefore, this value rather than the value obtained from the volumetric measurement has been used as a constraint for the total deuterium occupancy of the deuteride in the refinement of the neutron diffraction pattern. The oxygen and nitrogen contents in the sample detected by the oxygen and nitrogen determinator after the neutron and X-ray measurements are 0.5 and 0.4wt.%, respectively, which suggests that the oxidation and nitridation of the sample are not serious. The lattice parameters of the fcc lattices derived from the X-ray and neutron measurements are 4.407(3) and 4.406(3)Å, respectively. As the differences are within their standard deviations, we have performed simultaneous X-ray/neutron refinement for the data using the option of multiple X-ray and neutron histograms (datasets) provide by the LHPM program [8].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Deformation in interstitial deuterium sublattice and host alloy lattice
ce:section.ce:para: Since the neutron and X-ray diffraction techniques could individually reveal the structural details of the interstitial deuterium sublattice and the host Ti–Mo alloy lattice, it would be interesting to know how these two sublattices respond to a same action. In our analysis, we have studied the deformation effects of the two sublattices due to the entering deuterium. The interactions between hydrogen and its host lattice, and the relevant strain fields are fundamentally important for metal hydrogen systems. Some phenomenological theories of hydrogen–metal interaction dealing with the hydrogen-induced displacements in elastic continuum have been developed and the related experimental results have been reviewed [11]. It has been revealed from our Rietveld refinements that both the neutron and X-ray diffraction patterns show significant intrinsic line broadening. The revealed line broadening indicates a possible deformation effect due to the defects of the deuteride created by the entering deuterium during the deuterization process. The defects in a titanium alloy hydride could be very complicated. However, the most common defects in the hydrides of titanium alloys have been the dislocations [12,13]. Therefore a dislocation-induced lattice deformation model has been exploited to correct the line broadening of the neutron and X-ray diffraction patterns and describe the deformation effects of the interstitial deuterium sublattice and the host alloy lattice.

According to Ryaboshapka and Tikhonov [10], the dislocation-induced lattice deformation for an elastically anisotropic metal with fcc symmetry is described as:
ε
rms
2
= κ ( 1 − η Γ )
, where ɛ rms is the root-mean-square strain, κ a factor related to the elastic properties and dislocation features of the metal, η an anisotropy factor which is a function of the elastic moduli associated with the proper dislocations of the metals, and Γ is related to the diffraction vector and expressed by miller indices of the reflection planes as: Γ =(h 2 k 2 + h 2 l 2 + k 2 l 2)/(h 2 + k 2 + l 2)2. Therefore, generally the dislocations in a fcc lattice would create certain degrees of anisotropic deformation with the minimum deformation on the (h
h
h) planes, and the maximum one on the (h 00) planes. By a proportional relation between the deformation and the micro-strain broadening of the diffraction lines, the factors in description of the deformation can be treated as refinable parameters in the micro-strain broadening function of tan θ in a Rietveld program, and the dislocation-induced deformation of the crystal lattice can be determined.

In the refinements of the diffraction patterns using the standard line broadening corrections provided in the Rietveld programs, the effect of lattice deformation is often approximated by an isotropic broadening parameter in the Gaussian component, and that of crystallite size by a broadening parameter in the Lorentzian component of the Voigt function [14]. However the implementation of the above corrections could not properly fit the broadening profiles of the diffraction patterns from both X-ray and neutron measurements. The agreement factors of the refinements are relatively high, and the derived values of micro-strains and crystallite sizes are relatively divergent. However in addition to Gaussian components, if we also implement the isotropic micro-strain broadening corrections in Lorentzian components of the Voigt function, the total agreement factor for the refinements of both patterns will be improved by a reduction of more than 0.6% of the R wp values, but the crystallite size broadening will be reduced to nearly 0. It indicates that the effect of lattice deformation is the main source of the intrinsic broadening for the deuteride, and it could be derived from the micro-strain broadening correction in the Gaussian and Lorentzian components of the Voigt function. The implementation of the dislocation-induced anisotropic deformation model in the micro-strain line broadening corrections can further reduce the agreement factors of R wp by more than 0.2% for the refinements of the X-ray data, but with very little impact for the refinements of the neutron data (the reduction of R wp is in the level of 0.01%). Therefore, the final structural information for the deuteride shown in Table 1 are derived from the simultaneous X-ray/neutron refinements by implementation of the dislocation-induced anisotropic deformation model in the micro-strain broadening corrections of the X-ray data and the isotropic micro-strain broadening corrections in the neutron data respectively. As shown in Table 1, the derived micro-strain values from both X-ray and neutron measurements are in the same level and appear to be consistent. Based on the values of the derived micro-strains, we may reckon the average lattice deformation in the two sublattices of the deuteride as coherent. However, some considerable differences of the lattice deformation of the two sublatttices have been revealed from the micro-strains broadening correction refinements. According to Ryaboshapka and Tikhonov [10], the values of the anisotropy factor η for the typical edge dislocations in fcc copper and α-brass are 0.75 and 0.85, respectively. In our refinements of the X-ray data, the η value is 1.0, which indicates screw dislocation and is similar to that of α-brass. Therefore the features of the deformation for the host alloy lattice would follow that of the typical fcc metals and have remarkable anisotropy on different planes. However for the neutron data, a small η value of 0.3 could be derived from the refinement of the neutron pattern. For such a low η value, the difference of the maximum lattice deformation on the (h 00) planes and the minimum one on the (h
h
h) planes is less than 10%. It explains why there is much less improvement for implementation of the dislocation-induced anisotropic micro-strain broadening correction in the refinements of the neutron data, since that the deformation for the interstitial deuterium sublattice is basically isotropic. This phenomenon suggests that in comparison with the deformation of the host alloy lattice, the deformation of the interstitial deuterium sublattice has relaxed apparently due to the distribution of the vacancies in the unsaturated interstitial deuterium sublattice around the cores of the dislocations. The relaxation effect is like to be a general rule for the deformations of all nonstoichiometric metal hydrides, but appears to have not been revealed due to the overlaps of the diffraction signals of the interstitial deuterium sublattice and the host alloy lattice in normal neutron diffraction measurements.

The Ti–Mo alloy containing 33at.% of Mo forms a null matrix alloy for elastic coherent neuron scattering. The deuterides of the alloy containing ∼1.8 deuterium atom per alloy atom forms a titanium δ-hydride type of structure with the deuterium atoms in the tetrahedral interstitial sites.

Considerable intrinsic line broadening effect is revealed in the refinements of the diffraction patterns of the deuteride phase. The analysis of the line broadening suggests that the deuterization of the alloy causes strong lattice deformation, and the lattice deformation is likely to be dislocation induced. The average lattice deformation in the host alloy lattice and the interstitial deuterium sublattice of the deuterides appears to be coherent. In comparison with the deformation of the host alloy lattice, a relaxation effect is observed for the deformation of the interstitial deuterium sublattice.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We appreciate S.C. Wang in IMR, Prof. D.F. Chen and B.S. Zhang in CIAE, Dr. H.L. Du in Peking University for assistants in the experiments. The project is supported by NSFC under Grant No. 50371086. L.P. Guo acknowledge the support of K.C. Wong Education Foundation, Hong Kong.

Progress in Hydrogen Treatment of Materials

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: B.A. Hunter, C.J. Howard, LHPM, a Computer Program for Rietveld Analysis of X-ray and Neutron Powder Diffraction Patterns, ANSTO Report, 1998.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrogen in Metals. I. Topics in Applied Physics
